intro: July 1997. Apple is 90 days from bankruptcy. Market share is 3% and falling. The board just fired Gil Amelio, and a co-founder you exiled twelve years ago is back in the building as an 'advisor.' You take the chair he's circling.

realOutcome.summary: Jobs' 1997-1998 decisions became the most famous corporate turnaround in history. Every 'what actually happened' below is real.

decisions.scenario: Apple licenses Mac OS to clone makers like Power Computing. Clones are outselling Macs at the low end and cannibalizing your margins — but killing the program means war with partners and less market share.

choices.text: Kill the clone program. Buy out Power Computing.
choices.reveal: This is what Jobs did within weeks — paid $100M for Power Computing and shut the rest down. Margins recovered immediately. 'The clone business was killing Apple.'

choices.text: Expand licensing. Market share is everything.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You become a low-margin software vendor competing with Microsoft at Microsoft's own game. Apple's hardware business bleeds out by 2001.

choices.text: Keep it but raise the license fees sharply.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Clone makers sue, partners flee, and you get the war anyway — just slower and with worse press. Half-measures don't save companies 90 days from broke.

decisions.scenario: Microsoft — mid antitrust siege, needing Apple alive as proof of 'competition' — offers $150M investment plus a 5-year Office-for-Mac commitment. Your faithful will see it as surrender to the enemy.

choices.text: Take the deal. Announce it on the Boston keynote screen.
choices.reveal: Jobs did exactly this — Gates' giant face loomed over Macworld Boston as the crowd booed. It guaranteed Office on Mac, and the $150M signaled Apple would live. The stock jumped 33% that day.

choices.text: Refuse. Apple doesn't take charity from Redmond.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Microsoft quietly sunsets Office for Mac. Corporate buyers flee a platform with no spreadsheets. Pride is not a runway extension.

choices.text: Counter: demand $500M and IE off the Mac.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Gates, who needs this deal less than you do, walks. You spent your one lifeline negotiating for a better lifeline.

decisions.scenario: Apple ships 40+ overlapping products — Performas, Quadras, printers, the Newton. Engineers can't explain the lineup to their own families. Every product has a team, a VP, and a constituency.

choices.reveal: Jobs drew a 2x2 grid on a whiteboard and killed everything outside it — including the Newton. 70% of the product line died. Focus is saying no; the survivors became the iMac and PowerBook.

choices.text: Trim to 15 products gradually to protect morale.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Each product keeps its lobbying VP, the burn rate barely moves, and 'gradually' turns out to be a speed bankruptcy doesn't respect.

choices.text: Keep the lineup but cut every team's budget 30%.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Forty mediocre products become forty bad ones. You've distributed the death evenly instead of choosing what lives.

decisions.scenario: The board wants you as permanent CEO with a big salary and options package. Taking it means owning the turnaround publicly — and Pixar's board wants to know where your loyalty sits.

choices.text: Take 'interim CEO' at $1/year. Keep both jobs.
choices.reveal: Jobs stayed 'iCEO' at a $1 salary until 2000. The non-title bought him political cover to make brutal cuts, and the $1 salary became the most famous compensation package in business.

choices.text: Take the full CEO package. Commit completely.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: It works fine — but the optics of a rich-package CEO firing thousands buys you angrier headlines and a touchier board through the hard cuts.

choices.text: Decline. Recruit an outside CEO, stay advisor.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: The outside hire is Amelio with better hair. The product cuts stall in committee, and the guy who could have saved Apple watches from the advisor's seat.

decisions.scenario: Your ad budget is nearly gone. The agency pitches a brand campaign with zero product shots — just Einstein, Gandhi, and Dylan, and the line 'Think Different.' Marketing says you need to sell computers, not philosophy.

choices.text: Run it. Then bet the company on a translucent all-in-one.
choices.reveal: Think Different rebuilt the brand's soul; the Bondi-blue iMac (August 1998) became the fastest-selling computer in Apple history — 800,000 units in five months. The turnaround was real.

choices.text: Run product ads. Spec sheets and price points.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You win a few spec arguments against beige boxes with better specs and lower prices. Apple without a soul is just a worse Dell.

choices.text: Skip ads entirely. Put every dollar into R&D.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: The iMac still ships, but launches into silence. Great products don't sell themselves when the world has already written your obituary.

bands.line: You matched the greatest turnaround ever executed. Legend episode: WON.
Alive, but limping into the dot-com crash. No iPod money in this timeline.
Apple dies in 1998. Michael Dell said to shut it down and give the money back — you proved him right. 💀

endings.ctaLine: Apple 1000x'd after this fork. Today's agents flagged a company at a similar crossroads →
